questions.question:
The fundamental operations in a double-ended queue D are:
insertFirst(e) - Insert a new element at the beginning of D.
insertLast(e) - Insert a new element at the end of D.
removeFirst() - Remove and return the first element of D.
removeLast() - Remove and return the last element of D.

In an empty double-ended queue, the following operations are performed:
insertFirst(10)
insertLast(32)
a <- removeFirst()
insertLast(28)
insertLast(17)
a <- removeFirst()
a <- removeLast()

The value of a is ______
questions.to_find: The final value of variable a after the sequence of deque operations

understand.plain: A double-ended queue (deque) allows insertion and deletion from both front and rear. We start with an empty deque and perform a series of insertFirst, insertLast, removeFirst, and removeLast operations, updating a variable 'a' three times. We need to find the final value of 'a' after the last assignment.

keywords.explain: A data structure that supports insertion and deletion from both the front and the rear ends.
keywords.example: Deque: [10, 20, 30] — can insert/delete from both ends.

keywords.explain: removeFirst removes from the front of the deque; removeLast removes from the rear.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='280' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='260' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Deque State After Each Operation</text><text x='10' y='60' class='sub mut'>Step 1: insertFirst(10)</text><rect x='200' y='68' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='89' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10</text><text x='10' y='115' class='sub mut'>Step 2: insertLast(32)</text><rect x='200' y='123' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10</text><rect x='262' y='123' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='292' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>32</text><text x='10' y='170' class='sub mut'>Step 3: a <- removeFirst() => a=10</text><rect x='200' y='178' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='199' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>32</text><text x='10' y='225' class='sub mut'>Step 4: insertLast(28), insertLast(17)</text><rect x='200' y='233' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='254' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>32</text><rect x='262' y='233' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='292' y='254' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>28</text><rect x='324' y='233' width='60' height='32' class='bg'/><text x='354' y='254' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>17</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Deque state after each operation: after insertFirst(10) -> [10]; insertLast(32) -> [10,32]; a=removeFirst() -> a=10, deque=[32]; insertLast(28) -> [32,28]; insertLast(17) -> [32,28,17]; a=removeFirst() -> a=32, deque=[28,17]; a=removeLast() -> a=17, deque=[28].

given.aim: Trace through the deque operations and find the final value of variable a.

terms.meaning: Inserts element e at the front of the deque
terms.connects: Changes the front element of the deque

terms.meaning: Inserts element e at the rear of the deque
terms.connects: Changes the rear element of the deque

terms.meaning: Removes and returns the front/rear element of the deque
terms.connects: The last removeLast() call gives the final value of a

given.plan: Simulate the deque operations step by step, tracking the deque contents and the value of 'a' after each assignment. The last assignment to 'a' gives the answer.

steps.title: Simulate the first four operations
steps.apply:
Start: Deque = []
insertFirst(10): Deque = [10]
insertLast(32): Deque = [10, 32]
a <- removeFirst(): removes 10 from front, a = 10, Deque = [32]
insertLast(28): Deque = [32, 28]
steps.note: The deque is ordered front-to-rear; insertFirst adds at front, insertLast adds at rear.

steps.title: Simulate the remaining operations
steps.apply:
insertLast(17): Deque = [32, 28, 17]
a <- removeFirst(): removes 32 from front, a = 32, Deque = [28, 17]
a <- removeLast(): removes 17 from rear, a = 17, Deque = [28]
Final value of a = 17
steps.note: Each assignment to 'a' overwrites the previous value. The last assignment is a <- removeLast() which returns 17.

questions.formula_note: Direct simulation of double-ended queue (deque) operations: insertFirst, insertLast, removeFirst, removeLast.

questions.answer: B. Reverses the order of the elements in the queue Q
questions.to_find: The operation performed by the recursive function f on a queue

understand.plain: The function f recursively deletes the front element of a queue, calls itself on the remaining queue, and then inserts the deleted element back at the rear. By tracing through the recursion, we can determine what overall transformation is applied to the queue.

keywords.explain: A FIFO data structure where elements are inserted at the rear and deleted from the front.
The function removes the front element, recurses on the rest, then re-inserts the element. This causes elements to be inserted in reverse order of removal.
keywords.example: f([1,2,3]): removes 1, recurses f([2,3]), inserts 1 at rear -> [3,2,1].

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 320' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='320' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='250' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Recursion Trace for f([1,2,3])</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub mut'>Call 1: delete front=1, Q=[2,3], recurse</text><text x='20' y='90' class='sub mut'>  Call 2: delete front=2, Q=[3], recurse</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub mut'>    Call 3: delete front=3, Q=[], recurse</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub mut'>      Call 4: isEmpty=true, return</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub mut'>    Call 3 resumes: insert(Q,3), Q=[3]</text><text x='20' y='210' class='sub mut'>  Call 2 resumes: insert(Q,2), Q=[3,2]</text><text x='20' y='240' class='sub mut'>Call 1 resumes: insert(Q,1), Q=[3,2,1]</text><rect x='20' y='260' width='460' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='286' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Result: [3, 2, 1] — Queue is REVERSED</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion trace: f([1,2,3]) - delete 1, recurse on [2,3]; delete 2, recurse on [3]; delete 3, recurse on []; base case returns; insert 3 -> [3]; insert 2 -> [3,2]; insert 1 -> [3,2,1]. The queue is reversed.

given.aim: Determine what operation the recursive function f performs on a queue.

terms.meaning: Removes and returns the front element of the queue
terms.connects: Saves the front element into i before recursing

terms.meaning: Recursively processes the rest of the queue (all elements except the deleted front)
terms.example: After deleting 1 from [1,2,3], f is called on [2,3]
terms.connects: By induction, recursion reverses the sub-queue before we insert i

terms.meaning: Inserts element i at the rear of the queue after the recursion returns
terms.connects: The previously-front element is placed at the rear, achieving reversal

given.plan: Trace the recursion with a small example (e.g., Q=[1,2,3]). Observe that the recursive call first reverses the sub-queue, then the current front element is appended at the rear. By induction, the entire queue gets reversed.

steps.title: Understand the recursive structure
steps.formula_raw: f(Q): i = delete(front); f(remaining Q); insert(i) at rear
steps.apply: The function pops the front element i, recurses on the remaining queue, then inserts i at the rear. This is a classic reversal pattern: the front element ends up at the back, and recursion handles the rest.
steps.note: Base case: if Q is empty, nothing is done. Each element is removed in FIFO order and re-inserted in LIFO order (because the recursive call finishes before the insert).

steps.title: Trace with Q = [1, 2, 3]
steps.apply:
Call f([1,2,3]): i=1, recurse on [2,3]
  Call f([2,3]): i=2, recurse on [3]
    Call f([3]): i=3, recurse on []
      f([]) returns immediately (isEmpty)
    insert(Q,3): Q=[3]
  insert(Q,2): Q=[3,2]
insert(Q,1): Q=[3,2,1]
Original: [1,2,3] -> Result: [3,2,1] — reversed!
steps.note: Elements are removed front-to-back (1,2,3) and re-inserted in the same order, but because the insert happens AFTER the recursion returns, the last-removed element (3) is inserted first, then 2, then 1, giving reverse order.

solution.result: The function reverses the order of elements in the queue. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Recursive queue reversal: removing front elements and reinserting after recursive call produces a reversed queue, analogous to reversing a list using a stack.
